text: Working with APIs allows applications to communicate and share data over the internet. When you send an HTTP request, for example a GET or POST, the API usually responds with data formatted as JSON, which is easy for programs to read. Many APIs require authentication, often through an API key, to ensure only authorised users access the data. However, you must also respect rate limits, which restrict how many requests you can make in a certain time period. If you ignore these limits, the API may block your access. Finally, always include error handling in your code. This helps you catch and manage problems such as network failures or invalid responses gracefully.
